results.question: What was the total borrowings (short-term + long-term debt) for each of the last 5 financial years?
results.answer:
## Details

The total borrowings for each financial year are explicitly provided in the balance sheet data for the last 5 years as follows:

- FY2022 Borrowings: 98.30 Cr  
- FY2023 Borrowings: 87.10 Cr  
- FY2024 Borrowings: 119.40 Cr  
- FY2025 Borrowings: 233.20 Cr  
- FY2026 Borrowings: 250.30 Cr  

The data does not separately disclose short-term and long-term borrowings; only the aggregate borrowings figure is available.

## Summary

The total borrowings (short-term + long-term) for the last 5 financial years were:  
- FY2022: 98.30 Cr  
- FY2023: 87.10 Cr  
- FY2024: 119.40 Cr  
- FY2025: 233.20 Cr  
- FY2026: 250.30 Cr  

No separate breakdown into short-term and long-term borrowings is provided in the retrieved context.

results.question: What was the operating profit margin (operating profit / revenue × 100) for each of the last 5 financial years?
results.answer:
## Details

The operating profit margin is calculated as:

\[
\text{Operating Profit Margin} = \frac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100
\]

From the context, the formula used for the given EBITDA margin values is:

\[
\frac{\text{EBITDA} - \text{Depreciation}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100
\]

Since Operating Profit = EBITDA - Depreciation, the given values are effectively the operating profit margin for each year.

The values provided are:

- FY2022: 11.22%
- FY2023: 12.48%
- FY2024: 11.36%
- FY2025: 11.57%
- FY2026: 11.47%

These are directly the operating profit margins for the respective years.

## Summary

The operating profit margin for the last five financial years was:

- FY2022: 11.22%
- FY2023: 12.48%
- FY2024: 11.36%
- FY2025: 11.57%
- FY2026: 11.47%

## Sources

Financial Statements.xlsx · calculation · 2026

results.question: What was the year-on-year revenue growth rate for each of the last 4 financial years?
results.answer:
## Details

The last 4 financial years based on the data are FY2023, FY2024, FY2025, and FY2026. The sales (revenue) figures for these years from the profit & loss data are:

- FY2023 Sales: ₹5158.50 Cr  
- FY2024 Sales: ₹5641.40 Cr  
- FY2025 Sales: ₹5832.40 Cr  
- FY2026 Sales: ₹6568.60 Cr  

## Calculation

Year-on-year revenue growth rate formula:  
\[
\text{Growth Rate} = \frac{\text{Current Year Sales} - \text{Previous Year Sales}}{\text{Previous Year Sales}} \times 100
\]

1. Growth from FY2022 to FY2023:  
\[
\frac{5158.50 - 4394.00}{4394.00} \times 100 = \frac{764.50}{4394.00} \times 100 = 17.40\%
\]

2. Growth from FY2023 to FY2024:  
\[
\frac{5641.40 - 5158.50}{5158.50} \times 100 = \frac{482.90}{5158.50} \times 100 = 9.36\%
\]

3. Growth from FY2024 to FY2025:  
\[
\frac{5832.40 - 5641.40}{5641.40} \times 100 = \frac{191.00}{5641.40} \times 100 = 3.38\%
\]

4. Growth from FY2025 to FY2026:  
\[
\frac{6568.60 - 5832.40}{5832.40} \times 100 = \frac{736.20}{5832.40} \times 100 = 12.62\%
\]

## Summary

The year-on-year revenue growth rates for the last 4 financial years were:  
- FY2023: 17.40%  
- FY2024: 9.36%  
- FY2025: 3.38%  
- FY2026: 12.62%

## Sources

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2023  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2024  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2025  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2026

results.question: What was the free cash flow (operating cash flow minus capital expenditure) for each of the last 5 financial years?
results.answer:
## Details

Free cash flow (FCF) is calculated as:

**Free Cash Flow = Cash from Operating Activity - Capital Expenditure (Capex)**

From the retrieved context:

- Cash from Operating Activity for last 5 years (FY2022 to FY2026) is available from cash_flow sheets:
  - FY2022: 543.10 Cr
  - FY2023: 556.90 Cr
  - FY2024: 823.40 Cr
  - FY2025: 629.60 Cr
  - FY2026: 1117.00 Cr

- Capital Expenditure (Capex) is not explicitly stated in the cash flow or balance sheet data. However, Capex is typically reflected in the cash flow from investing activities as outflows related to purchase of fixed assets.

- Cash from Investing Activity for last 5 years:
  - FY2022: +71.50 Cr (positive inflow)
  - FY2023: -478.10 Cr
  - FY2024: -541.00 Cr
  - FY2025: -512.60 Cr
  - FY2026: -506.50 Cr

Since cash from investing activity includes all investing cash flows (purchase/sale of fixed assets, investments, etc.), and no separate Capex line is provided, we cannot isolate exact Capex.

- Net Block and Capital Work in Progress (CWIP) from balance sheet can indicate investment in fixed assets but do not provide exact cash outflow amounts.

**Important:** The exact Capex figure is not explicitly disclosed in the retrieved context for any year.

## Summary

The free cash flow for the last 5 financial years cannot be precisely calculated because the exact capital expenditure (Capex) figures are not disclosed in the retrieved context. Only cash from operating activity and cash from investing activity are available, but investing activity includes other items besides Capex, so it cannot be used as a proxy.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · cash_flow · FY2022 to FY2026  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2022 to FY2026

results.question: What were the receivable days, inventory days, and payable days for each of the last 3 financial years?
results.expected_answer: FY24: [Receivable: 24.3, Inventory: 85.4], FY25: [Receivable: 27.2, Inventory: 95.6], FY26: [Receivable: 26.4, Inventory: 102.3]. Payable days not computable from available data — no Trade Payables line item in this dataset.
results.answer:
## Details

To calculate receivable days, inventory days, and payable days for the last 3 financial years (FY2024, FY2025, FY2026), we need the following data for each year:

- Receivables (from Balance Sheet)
- Inventory (from Balance Sheet)
- Payables (specifically "Trade Payables" or "Current Liabilities - Trade Payables" if available)
- Sales (from Profit & Loss)
- Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) or Raw Material Cost + Change in Inventory + Other manufacturing expenses (to approximate COGS if exact COGS is not given)
- Purchases or Cost of Goods Sold (for Payables days calculation)

**Important:**  
- The exact line item "Trade Payables" or "Current Liabilities - Trade Payables" is NOT disclosed in the retrieved context. Only "Other Liabilities" and "Borrowings" are given, but these cannot be used as proxies for payables per Rule 2.  
- Therefore, payable days cannot be calculated due to lack of payables data.

### Data extracted for FY2024, FY2025, FY2026:

| Metric               | FY2024           | FY2025           | FY2026           |
|----------------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Sales (Cr)           | 5641.40          | 5832.40          | 6568.60          |
| Raw Material Cost (Cr)| 3435.80          | 3426.00          | 3968.20          |
| Change in Inventory (Cr) | 86.00         | 27.80            | 17.10            |
| Other Mfr. Exp (Cr)  | 252.40           | 274.80           | Not disclosed (only "Other Expenses" 965.20 Cr in FY26, but not specified as manufacturing) |
| Receivables (Cr)     | 375.80           | 435.30           | 475.10           |
| Inventory (Cr)       | 913.40           | 1011.10          | 1117.30          |

- Payables data is not disclosed.

### Calculation approach:

- **Receivable Days** = (Receivables / Sales) * 365  
- **Inventory Days** = (Inventory / Cost of Goods Sold) * 365  
- **Payable Days** = Cannot be calculated (payables not disclosed)

**Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) approximation:**  
Since exact COGS is not given, we use:

COGS = Raw Material Cost + Other Manufacturing Expenses - Change in Inventory

For FY2026, "Other Mfr. Exp" is not explicitly given, only "Other Expenses" (965.20 Cr) which is too broad and cannot be used as a proxy per Rule 2. So for FY2026, we will only use Raw Material Cost - Change in Inventory as COGS approximation, noting this limitation.

## Calculation

### FY2024

- Receivable Days = (375.80 / 5641.40) * 365 = 0.0666 * 365 = 24.3 days
- COGS = Raw Material Cost + Other Mfr. Exp - Change in Inventory  
= 3435.80 + 252.40 - 86.00 = 3602.20 Cr  
- Inventory Days = (Inventory / COGS) * 365 = (913.40 / 3602.20) * 365 = 0.2537 * 365 = 92.6 days

### FY2025

- Receivable Days = (435.30 / 5832.40) * 365 = 0.0746 * 365 = 27.2 days
- COGS = 3426.00 + 274.80 - 27.80 = 3673.00 Cr
- Inventory Days = (1011.10 / 3673.00) * 365 = 0.2753 * 365 = 100.4 days

### FY2026

- Receivable Days = (475.10 / 6568.60) * 365 = 0.0723 * 365 = 26.4 days
- Other Mfr. Exp not disclosed for FY26, so COGS approximated as Raw Material Cost - Change in Inventory = 3968.20 - 17.10 = 3951.10 Cr  
- Inventory Days = (1117.30 / 3951.10) * 365 = 0.2829 * 365 = 103.3 days

### Payable Days

- Payables or Trade Payables line item is not disclosed in the retrieved context for FY2024, FY2025, or FY2026.  
- Therefore, payable days cannot be calculated.

## Summary

For the last three financial years, the receivable days and inventory days were:

- FY2024: Receivable Days = 24.3 days, Inventory Days = 92.6 days  
- FY2025: Receivable Days = 27.2 days, Inventory Days = 100.4 days  
- FY2026: Receivable Days = 26.4 days, Inventory Days = 103.3 days  

Payable days cannot be calculated as the required payables data is not disclosed in the retrieved context.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2024, FY2025, FY2026  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2024, FY2025, FY2026

results.question: What was the total capex spent over the last 5 financial years combined, and what was the cumulative revenue growth over the same period? Did capex translate into proportional top-line growth?
results.expected_answer: Total Capex (proxy from Investing CF): 2038.20, Cumulative Revenue Growth: 49.49%. FY22 had net investing inflow of 71.5 (excluded from capex sum). Capex of 2038 Cr drove only 49.5% revenue growth — relatively capital-intensive expansion.
results.answer:
## Details

### Capex over last 5 financial years (FY21 to FY25)

The exact capex figures for each year are not explicitly provided in the retrieved context from any source (Excel financials, Annual Report text, or earnings call transcripts). The context mentions ongoing expansions and new plants becoming operational (e.g., Hyderabad plant fully operational in FY25, Kanpur plant nearing completion), but no explicit capex amounts are disclosed.

### Revenue figures for last 5 financial years (FY21 to FY25) from Excel data:

- FY21 Sales: ₹3,176.30 Cr
- FY22 Sales: ₹4,394.00 Cr
- FY23 Sales: ₹5,158.50 Cr
- FY24 Sales: ₹5,641.40 Cr
- FY25 Sales: ₹5,832.40 Cr

### Cumulative revenue growth calculation:

- Baseline (FY21) = ₹3,176.30 Cr
- Ending (FY25) = ₹5,832.40 Cr

Revenue growth = ((Ending - Baseline) / Baseline) × 100  
= ((5,832.40 - 3,176.30) / 3,176.30) × 100  
= (2,656.10 / 3,176.30) × 100 ≈ 83.6%

### Capex to revenue growth proportionality:

- Since capex amounts are not disclosed, it is not possible to quantitatively assess whether capex translated into proportional top-line growth.
- The narrative from the earnings call and annual report mentions multiple plant expansions and capacity increases over these years, which likely supported revenue growth.
- However, without explicit capex figures, no direct proportionality or ratio can be established.

## Summary

The cumulative revenue growth over the last 5 financial years (FY21 to FY25) was approximately 83.6%, increasing from ₹3,176.30 Cr to ₹5,832.40 Cr. However, the total capex spent over this period is not disclosed in the retrieved context, so it is not possible to determine if the capex translated into proportional top-line growth.

Astral Analyst Meet,2025
23rd May,2025
Mr. Sandeep Engineer Speaks
So, we welcome you all to this fiscal year-end results discussion, and we’ll also talk about the
future and scenarios. We’ll answer all your questions in person. So, welcome all of you.
Actually, the range is so vast and significant that we opted for a bigger hall so that everybody
can see the complete range of product lines. We are also having a dealer meet thereafter from
8 PM in the same hall for Mumbai.
This year has been a very challenging year. The questions I’ve been answering since morning,
especially about the polymer industry, indicate that everyone faced a big, challenging year
and PVC was the most challenging polymer, where price challenges were prominent, followed
by growth challenges. But in this scenario, as you can see, across all four quarters, we have
delivered very good results. The growth has been modest, but the margins have been well-
maintained and improved.
From this, you can see many questions were asked about our CPVC growth. We don’t want to
quantify it people have quantified their numbers in percentages, above a certain percentage
but we don’t want to quantify the numbers. If you look at the numbers and the margins,
obviously, the market share and the growth of value-added products have gone up. CPVC is
one of the value-added products, as are the new products. If people have quantified the
numbers with huge growth, we don’t see any value in quantified numbers that reflect in value
addition. In short, I’m saying we have achieved growth in our core product lines and new
product lines, and we’ve done this with healthy margins.
As usual, I don’t want to go into a presentation or discuss capex cycles because this will be
covered in value terms. Mr. Savlani will take you through these numbers. But every plant has
a balance of expansion going on, including new plants. The Hyderabad plant is fully
operational, and this year, we will have the full range of pipe products manufactured from the
Hyderabad plant. The Kanpur plant is on the verge of completion in the construction segment,
and machines will start arriving from the next quarter. So, in the third quarter, the Kanpur
plant will be operational, and by year-end, it will be fully operational.
We have started fitting operations in the South and also in Rajasthan. We will shortly start
fitting operations in the East, in Orissa. There are also expansion plants in progress, so we can
easily cater to those markets for pipes as well as fittings. Our new range of valves, especially
ball valves, is complete. We are adding butterfly valves and other types of valves used in the
plumbing segment. This range of specialized valve products will be completed by this fiscal
year.
We are also adding machines for our silent pipe production. We have almost doubled the
capacity, even more than doubled. Our Drain Pro, which is doing well excellently, has also

results.question: In which financial year did the gap between EBITDA and operating cash flow diverge the most, and by how much?
results.expected_answer: Year: FY25, Divergence: 357.60. EBITDA (PBT + Interest + Depreciation) = 987.20, OCF = 629.60, Gap = 357.60
results.answer:
## Details

We need to find the year in which the difference (gap) between EBITDA and Cash from Operating Activity (Operating Cash Flow) was the largest in absolute terms.

From the Excel data:

### EBITDA (₹ Cr)
- FY2017: 258.62
- FY2018: 295.43
- FY2019: 339.50
- FY2020: 372.20
- FY2021: 686.80
- FY2022: 620.10
- FY2023: 821.70
- FY2024: 838.60
- FY2025: 918.10
- FY2026: 1044.80

### Cash from Operating Activity (₹ Cr)
- FY2017: 114.19
- FY2018: 281.85
- FY2019: 342.00
- FY2020: 405.40
- FY2021: 664.10
- FY2022: 543.10
- FY2023: 556.90
- FY2024: 823.40
- FY2025: 629.60
- FY2026: 1117.00

### Calculate the absolute gap (|EBITDA - Operating Cash Flow|) for each year:

- FY2017: |258.62 - 114.19| = 144.43
- FY2018: |295.43 - 281.85| = 13.58
- FY2019: |339.50 - 342.00| = 2.50
- FY2020: |372.20 - 405.40| = 33.20
- FY2021: |686.80 - 664.10| = 22.70
- FY2022: |620.10 - 543.10| = 77.00
- FY2023: |821.70 - 556.90| = 264.80
- FY2024: |838.60 - 823.40| = 15.20
- FY2025: |918.10 - 629.60| = 288.50
- FY2026: |1044.80 - 1117.00| = 72.20

## Summary

The largest divergence between EBITDA and operating cash flow occurred in FY2025, with a gap of ₹288.50 Cr.

results.question: What is the 3-year CAGR of revenue and the 3-year CAGR of total assets? Is the company growing revenue faster or slower than its asset base is expanding?
results.expected_answer: Revenue 3Y CAGR: 8.39, Asset 3Y CAGR: 10.04. Revenue is growing SLOWER than asset base — indicating declining asset utilization efficiency.
results.answer:
## Details

To calculate the 3-year CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) for revenue and total assets, we need the revenue and total assets values for the base year (3 years ago) and the ending year (most recent year).

- Latest year available: FY2026
- Base year for 3-year CAGR: FY2023

### Revenue (Sales) figures from profit_loss sheet:
- FY2023 Sales = 5158.50 Cr
- FY2026 Sales = 6568.60 Cr

### Total Assets figures from balance_sheet sheet:
Total Assets = Sum of all assets components disclosed. The balance sheet provides:
- Net Block
- Capital Work in Progress
- Other Assets
- Receivables
- Inventory
- Cash & Bank

We sum these components for each year to get total assets.

**FY2023 Total Assets:**
- Net Block = 1950.50 Cr
- Capital Work in Progress = 126.10 Cr
- Other Assets = 2285.50 Cr
- Receivables = 354.50 Cr
- Inventory = 874.60 Cr
- Cash & Bank = 682.10 Cr

Sum = 1950.50 + 126.10 + 2285.50 + 354.50 + 874.60 + 682.10 = 6273.30 Cr

**FY2026 Total Assets:**
- Net Block = 2945.00 Cr
- Capital Work in Progress = 88.70 Cr
- Other Assets = 2778.20 Cr
- Receivables = 475.10 Cr
- Inventory = 1117.30 Cr
- Cash & Bank = 943.40 Cr

Sum = 2945.00 + 88.70 + 2778.20 + 475.10 + 1117.30 + 943.40 = 8347.70 Cr

### CAGR Formula:
\[
\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{\text{Ending Value}}{\text{Beginning Value}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
\]
where \( n = 3 \) years.

## Calculation

### Revenue CAGR:
\[
= \left(\frac{6568.60}{5158.50}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} - 1
= (1.273)^{0.3333} - 1
\]

Calculate \( (1.273)^{0.3333} \):

Using a calculator:
\[
(1.273)^{0.3333} \approx 1.083
\]

So,
\[
\text{Revenue CAGR} = 1.083 - 1 = 0.083 = 8.3\%
\]

### Total Assets CAGR:
\[
= \left(\frac{8347.70}{6273.30}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} - 1
= (1.331)^{0.3333} - 1
\]

Calculate \( (1.331)^{0.3333} \):

Using a calculator:
\[
(1.331)^{0.3333} \approx 1.100
\]

So,
\[
\text{Total Assets CAGR} = 1.100 - 1 = 0.100 = 10.0\%
\]

## Summary

The 3-year CAGR of revenue from FY2023 to FY2026 is approximately 8.3%, while the 3-year CAGR of total assets over the same period is approximately 10.0%. Therefore, the company's asset base is expanding faster than its revenue growth.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2023, FY2026  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2023, FY2026

results.question: What is the current ratio for each of the most recent 3 financial years?
results.answer:
## Details

The current ratio is calculated as:

**Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities**

From the balance sheet data, the components of current assets and current liabilities must be identified exactly as per the line items provided. The retrieved context provides the following relevant line items for the most recent 3 financial years (FY2024, FY2025, FY2026):

### Current Assets components available:
- Receivables
- Inventory
- Cash & Bank

No other current asset line items are explicitly disclosed.

### Current Liabilities components available:
- Borrowings (not specified as current or non-current)
- Other Liabilities (not specified as current or non-current)

Since the exact line item "Current Liabilities" is not disclosed, and no breakdown of borrowings or other liabilities into current and non-current portions is provided, the exact current liabilities figure is not available.

### Data for FY2024, FY2025, FY2026:

| Year | Receivables (Cr) | Inventory (Cr) | Cash & Bank (Cr) | Borrowings (Cr) | Other Liabilities (Cr) |
|-------|------------------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|-----------------------|
| FY2024 | 375.80           | 913.40         | 609.60           | 119.40          | 1188.30               |
| FY2025 | 435.30           | 1011.10        | 608.30           | 233.20          | 1197.60               |
| FY2026 | 475.10           | 1117.30        | 943.40           | 250.30          | 1503.70               |

### Analysis:

- Total current assets can be summed as: Receivables + Inventory + Cash & Bank
- Current liabilities are not explicitly disclosed; borrowings and other liabilities are total figures without current/non-current split.

Because the exact "Current Liabilities" line item is not disclosed, the current ratio cannot be precisely calculated.

## Summary

The exact current ratio for FY2024, FY2025, and FY2026 cannot be computed because the required line item "Current Liabilities" is not disclosed in the retrieved context. While current assets components (Receivables, Inventory, Cash & Bank) are available, the absence of a clear current liabilities figure prevents calculation of the current ratio.

results.question: What is the return on capital employed (EBIT / capital employed × 100) for each of the most recent 3 financial years?
results.answer:
## Details

Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) is calculated as:

\[
\text{ROCE} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100
\]

Where:
- EBIT = Profit before interest and tax = Profit before tax + Interest (since PBT is after interest)
- Capital Employed = Total Assets - Current Liabilities or alternatively Net Worth + Borrowings (if current liabilities are not given)

From the retrieved context:

### Step 1: Extract EBIT for FY2024, FY2025, FY2026

- FY2024:
  - Profit before tax = 733.60 Cr
  - Interest = 29.10 Cr
  - EBIT = 733.60 + 29.10 = 762.70 Cr

- FY2025:
  - Profit before tax = 702.50 Cr
  - Interest = 41.30 Cr
  - EBIT = 702.50 + 41.30 = 743.80 Cr

- FY2026:
  - Profit before tax = 730.60 Cr
  - Interest = 64.40 Cr
  - EBIT = 730.60 + 64.40 = 795.00 Cr

### Step 2: Extract Capital Employed for FY2024, FY2025, FY2026

Capital Employed = Net Worth + Borrowings (since current liabilities are not explicitly given)

- FY2024:
  - Net Worth = 3188.10 Cr
  - Borrowings = 119.40 Cr
  - Capital Employed = 3188.10 + 119.40 = 3307.50 Cr

- FY2025:
  - Net Worth = 3617.00 Cr
  - Borrowings = 233.20 Cr
  - Capital Employed = 3617.00 + 233.20 = 3850.20 Cr

- FY2026:
  - Net Worth = 4057.90 Cr
  - Borrowings = 250.30 Cr
  - Capital Employed = 4057.90 + 250.30 = 4308.20 Cr

## Calculation

\[
\text{ROCE}_{FY2024} = \frac{762.70}{3307.50} \times 100 = 23.05\%
\]

\[
\text{ROCE}_{FY2025} = \frac{743.80}{3850.20} \times 100 = 19.32\%
\]

\[
\text{ROCE}_{FY2026} = \frac{795.00}{4308.20} \times 100 = 18.46\%
\]

## Summary

The Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) for Astral Ltd for the most recent three financial years are:

- FY2024: 23.05%
- FY2025: 19.32%
- FY2026: 18.46%

This shows a declining trend in ROCE over these three years.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2024, FY2025, FY2026  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2024, FY2025, FY2026

results.question: What is the interest coverage ratio (EBIT / interest expense) for each of the most recent 3 financial years?
results.answer:
## Details

The interest coverage ratio is defined as EBIT divided by interest expense. EBIT can be derived as EBITDA minus Depreciation.

From the provided data:

- FY2024:
  - EBITDA = 838.6
  - Depreciation = 197.6
  - Interest = 29.1
  - EBIT = 838.6 - 197.6 = 641.0

- FY2025:
  - EBITDA = 918.1
  - Depreciation = 243.4
  - Interest = 41.3
  - EBIT = 918.1 - 243.4 = 674.7

- FY2026:
  - EBITDA = 1044.8
  - Depreciation = 291.6
  - Interest = 64.4
  - EBIT = 1044.8 - 291.6 = 753.2

## Calculation

Interest coverage ratio = EBIT / Interest

- FY2024: 641.0 / 29.1 = 22.03
- FY2025: 674.7 / 41.3 = 16.34
- FY2026: 753.2 / 64.4 = 11.7

These match the ratios already provided in the context.

## Summary

The interest coverage ratios for the most recent three financial years are 22.03 for FY2024, 16.34 for FY2025, and 11.7 for FY2026, showing a declining trend over the period.

## Sources

Financial Statements.xlsx · calculation · 2026

results.question: Perform a DuPont decomposition of ROE for the most recent financial year. What are the three components: net profit margin (net profit / revenue), asset turnover (revenue / total assets), and equity multiplier (total assets / shareholders' equity)?
results.expected_answer: Net Profit Margin: 8.14%, Asset Turnover: 1.13, Equity Multiplier: 1.43, DuPont ROE: 13.18%
results.answer:
## Details

From the provided context, we have the following data for FY2026:

- Net profit = 534.7 (from profit_loss, FY2026)
- Sales (Revenue) = 6568.6 (from profit_loss, FY2026)

The DuPont decomposition requires three components:

1. **Net Profit Margin** = Net profit / Revenue  
   Using values from FY2026:  
   Net profit margin = 534.7 / 6568.6

2. **Asset Turnover** = Revenue / Total Assets  
   Total Assets value is required but **not provided** in the context.

3. **Equity Multiplier** = Total Assets / Shareholders' Equity  
   Both Total Assets and Shareholders' Equity values are required but **not provided** in the context.

Since Total Assets and Shareholders' Equity are not disclosed in the retrieved context, the asset turnover and equity multiplier cannot be calculated.

## Calculation

Net Profit Margin = 534.7 / 6568.6 = 0.0814 (approx)

Asset Turnover = Not computable (Total Assets not disclosed)

Equity Multiplier = Not computable (Total Assets and Shareholders' Equity not disclosed)

## Summary

For FY2026, the net profit margin is approximately 0.0814. However, the asset turnover and equity multiplier components of the DuPont decomposition cannot be calculated because total assets and shareholders' equity figures are not provided in the available data.

## Sources

Financial Statements.xlsx · calculation · 2026

results.question: What is the cash conversion cycle for each of the most recent 2 financial years? Calculate receivable days, inventory days, and payable days separately and show the net cycle.
results.expected_answer: FY25: Receivable Days: 27.24, Inventory Days: 95.57, Partial CCC (excl Payable Days): 122.81. FY26: Receivable Days: 26.40, Inventory Days: 102.33, Partial CCC (excl Payable Days): 128.73. Payable Days not computable — no Trade Payables line item in available data.
results.answer:
## Details

The question requires calculation of the cash conversion cycle (CCC) for the two most recent financial years, which involves:

- Receivable Days = (Trade Receivables / Revenue) × 365
- Inventory Days = (Inventory / Cost of Goods Sold) × 365
- Payable Days = (Trade Payables / Cost of Goods Sold) × 365
- Cash Conversion Cycle = Receivable Days + Inventory Days - Payable Days

However, the provided context only includes data related to Cash from Operating Activity and Net Profit for FY2025 and FY2026. There is no information on:

- Trade Receivables
- Inventory
- Trade Payables
- Revenue
- Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)

Since these line items are not disclosed in the retrieved context, it is not possible to calculate receivable days, inventory days, payable days, or the net cash conversion cycle for FY2025 or FY2026.

## Summary

The cash conversion cycle and its components (receivable days, inventory days, payable days) cannot be calculated because the required line items such as trade receivables, inventory, trade payables, revenue, and cost of goods sold are not provided in the available data.

## Sources

Financial Statements.xlsx · calculation · 2026

results.question: What is the operating cash flow to net profit ratio for each of the last 3 financial years? Is the company consistently converting its accounting profits into real cash?
results.expected_answer: FY24: 1.51, FY25: 1.20, FY26: 2.09. Yes, OCF consistently exceeds net profit across all 3 years, indicating strong cash conversion.
results.answer:
## Details

The question asks for the operating cash flow to net profit ratio for the last 3 financial years and whether the company is consistently converting accounting profits into real cash.

- The last 3 financial years based on the data are FY2026, FY2025, and FY2024.
- Net profit figures from Excel profit_loss sheet:
  - FY2026 Net Profit = ₹534.70 Cr
  - FY2025 Net Profit = ₹523.80 Cr
  - FY2024 Net Profit = ₹546.10 Cr

- Operating cash flow figures are not explicitly provided in the Excel profit_loss data.
- The Annual Report text (pages 110 and 168 of FY25 Annual Report) contains sections on cash flows but the exact operating cash flow numbers for FY25, FY24, and FY26 are not explicitly stated in the retrieved context.
- The earnings call transcript and annual report excerpts mention cash generated from operations and working capital changes but do not provide explicit operating cash flow amounts for these years.
- Without explicit operating cash flow figures for FY24, FY25, and FY26, the ratio cannot be calculated.

## Summary

The operating cash flow to net profit ratio for FY2024, FY2025, and FY2026 cannot be calculated because the exact operating cash flow figures for these years are not available in the retrieved context. Therefore, it is not possible to conclusively determine from the provided data whether the company is consistently converting its accounting profits into real cash.

This is the plumbing business working capital cycle. A similar graph, mainly because the creditor payoff
taken place in the plumbing business only. Because of that, you’re seeing an increase in net working
capital days. But that has come down again in the month of May. Nothing to worry about on that side
it was about taking advantage of the currency.
This is the graph to understand the PVC price trend. You can see that PVC is continuously falling in a
one-way journey. Except last year in April-May, there was a spike in PVC, and that’s why you see in the
last year’s first quarter, the volume growth across industries was very good. We were also close to 16%
volume growth, and then it started a southward journey. This is a continuous falling graph, and last
year alone, the polymer price was down by 18%. Despite a 18% fall, you can see that in this
environment, Astral is the only company in the country that has improved its gross profit margin. We
have improved the GP by 1%, and we have maintained our EBITDA.
So this is the power of the brand. I can say that we have established ourselves in the market, where
people respect the brand, and people are ready to pay us for the quality. Many players have tried a lot
of things by undercutting the value and throwing the material in the market with material, but even
after that, they were not able to grow their volumes. So, customers understand that quality players
will always command a premium, and that’s what we are always communicating that we believe in
consistency. We don’t want zigzag growth. We want to maintain consistency in terms of growth and
margins as well. And that’s what we are doing right now. There were a lot of pressures on us too, with
competitors selling at lower prices, and now they are all suffering. Many of their projects are getting
blacklisted as well. We don’t want to name anyone, but everyone has started facing this problem in
the market.
This is a graph of paint and adhesive. You can see the last five years' growth; you can see 21.23% but
that’s not the correct graph because paint was added at a later stage. So, it may not be the right graph.
The EBITDA growth is 8.55%, and the profit before tax (PBT) is low because we have taken a lot of
amortizations that’s why it is showing very low Over the last three years, if you see, we have made a
lot of corrections. New additions are taking place for dealers, and because of that, EBITDA is under
pressure, particularly in the paint business. But now, I think, once our volumes start picking up from
this year itself, you will see a lot of improvement in this as well.
As far as the adhesive business is concerned, I think we are consistently growing at a 17 t0 18 % for
the last 11 years from where we have started our journey in 2014 and when we started that time our
EBITDA percentage was just 6% now from 6% journey to this year full year number we are close to
17% I am not talking about the last quarter where we had a closed to about 19% EBITDA but that was
was the peak quarter so because of that it was high but otherwise on a full year there wages also we
were close to about 17%. So adhesive business of India is continuously doing well. Even UK was also
doing great for us because we are having access to the new technology from the UK and we are getting
lot of inputs from our UK unit. It is unfortunate that the last year was the abnormal year for the UK
company. Otherwise last 10 year we have seen a good number. We started journey from almost ₹70-
80 crore top line in UK. And from 80 crore top line to this year we close with 346 so almost you can say
3X more than 3X in the span of just an year. So UK has also deliver a good number. This was the only
exceptional year and we are confident that we will be delivering the good number from the UK also
from the current year itself.
So, this is a very small letter. So we are going to share this presentation to you. So you can go through
that also. So what is the industry outlook? What we are saying? So we we are of the view that the

fittings approved by German authorities for gas application lines. They also have fittings that
can be used for water lines, Jal Sena projects, various projects, and industrial piping systems.
Now we are getting four machines—three are coming, and one more will come later—for
making PE and PP pipes in our plants. The reason behind Al Aziz was to enter this product line
with a complete range of products, including pipes and fittings.
PTMT has also started getting a good response, and you can see the range here. So, I think we
are covering all the products, all the ranges, and we would be happier to answer a lot of
queries you have in mind. Because of this challenging year that passed, the businesses we
have extended—especially the paint, bathware business, and the new polymer businesses we
have entered into thank you, everyone, for coming here. Thank you for attending one more
this analyst meets after the fiscal year. If you have more questions, we will be available. Kairav
is here, my elder son; Hiranand Bhai, our Director and CFO; and Saumya is here, the younger
son. Thank you very much to all of you, and our team is here to answer your questions. Thank
you, everyone.
Mr. Hiranand Savlani Speaks
Good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Astral’s analyst meet. The results are in front of you, so I won’t
discuss individual numbers much. We have a few slides to present, and then we will give maximum
time to the question-and-answer session. This is the consolidated full-year performance. You can see
the last five-year revenue growth CAGR is 16.5%, and similarly, EBITDA growth is 10.48%. PBT is 7.15%.
PBT is mainly low because we have taken a lot of hits on the amortization of the new businesses which
we acquired. To take the tax advantage, we amortized a lot of things, so because of that, the PBT
numbers are low. Otherwise, cash profit, you will see, is more or less similar to the EBITDA level.
This is the working capital cycle. In the month of March, the rupee appreciated, so we paid off a lot of
creditors early to take advantage of the currency. Because of that, the creditor levels have gone down.
Otherwise, you can see that the debtor days and inventory days are more or less in the similar line,
with two to three days here and there. But that has come down in the month of April.
This is the cash position. Right now, we are sitting on a net cash of ₹464 crore. This is the revenue
breakup of our pipe plumbing—it’s still the pipe business contributing 72%, and paint and adhesive
contributing 28%. In the coming time, you will see this ratio will be a little toward the paint and
adhesive side because the base is very low.
This is the capacity chart. You can see that the capacity has been continuously growing. Though We
might not require that much capacity, but we are still continuously pumping money into capex. The
reason is simple that we want to decentralize our units, and we want to be a pan-India operation,
considering our vision for the next 10 to 15 years. Because if we don’t decentralize our units, with
polymer prices coming down day by day, the element of freight cost is increasing in terms of
percentage. In that case, the more and more decentralized you are, the more you will get the
advantage of logistics costs. Considering that, we are continuously adding capacity. You might see our
average utilization in the 55 to 65 range, but once our decentralization is over, you will see a sizable
improvement. In the last two years alone, we have pumped in close to ₹1000 crore into this expansion
activity, particularly capex. But that benefit you will get in the next five years. So, capacity building is
taking place at a 10.3% CAGR, while sales are happening at a 13.5% CAGR. Capacity utilization is
continuously improving, but it’s still not up to the mark because we are decentralizing that thing.

So I think we can take any other questions. And thank you everyone. For joining us for this end of
this fiscal understanding from us. And discussing how the future is going to shape up for the years to
for this next fiscal. And everything is great Bright. And we are keeping on the hard work. And looking
forward again to meet you at the end of the next fiscal. Thank you very much everyone. Thank you.
Thank you.

results.question: What is the EBIT-to-interest-expense ratio and the change in total borrowings for the most recent 2 financial years? Is the company comfortably servicing its debt while reducing or maintaining its debt levels?
results.expected_answer: FY25: ICR=18.01x, Borrowings change: +113.80 (119.4 to 233.2). FY26: ICR=12.34x, Borrowings change: +17.10 (233.2 to 250.3). ICR is comfortable but declining. Borrowings increased significantly in FY25, then stabilized in FY26.
results.answer:
## Details

### EBIT-to-Interest-Expense Ratio

- EBIT is not directly given but can be derived as:  
  EBIT = Profit before tax + Interest expense (since PBT = EBIT - Interest)  
- For FY2025:  
  - Profit before tax = ₹702.50 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25)  
  - Interest expense = ₹41.30 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25)  
  - EBIT = 702.50 + 41.30 = ₹743.80 Cr  

- For FY2024:  
  - Profit before tax = ₹733.60 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY24)  
  - Interest expense = ₹29.10 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY24)  
  - EBIT = 733.60 + 29.10 = ₹762.70 Cr  

- EBIT-to-Interest-Expense Ratio = EBIT / Interest Expense  
  - FY2025 = 743.80 / 41.30 ≈ 18.01  
  - FY2024 = 762.70 / 29.10 ≈ 26.20  

### Change in Total Borrowings

- Total borrowings as per Annual Report notes (in ₹ Million):  
  - As at March 31, 2025 = ₹784 Million (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 154 · FY25)  
  - As at March 31, 2024 = ₹300 Million (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 154 · FY25)  

- Change in borrowings = 784 - 300 = ₹484 Million increase  

### Debt Servicing and Debt Levels

- EBIT-to-interest ratio is very strong in both years (FY25: ~18, FY24: ~26), indicating comfortable interest coverage.  
- However, total borrowings increased significantly by ₹484 Million from FY24 to FY25, indicating the company has increased its debt levels rather than reducing or maintaining them.  
- From the earnings call transcript and annual report notes, there is no indication of distress in debt servicing; the company maintains liquidity and manages working capital well.  

## Summary

The EBIT-to-interest-expense ratio declined from approximately 26.20 in FY24 to 18.01 in FY25 but remains comfortably high, indicating strong ability to service interest costs. However, total borrowings increased from ₹300 million in FY24 to ₹784 million in FY25, showing the company has increased its debt levels rather than reducing or maintaining them. Despite higher borrowings, the company appears to be comfortably servicing its debt.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY24  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 154 · FY25  
AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Page 19 · FY25

This is the plumbing business working capital cycle. A similar graph, mainly because the creditor payoff
taken place in the plumbing business only. Because of that, you’re seeing an increase in net working
capital days. But that has come down again in the month of May. Nothing to worry about on that side
it was about taking advantage of the currency.
This is the graph to understand the PVC price trend. You can see that PVC is continuously falling in a
one-way journey. Except last year in April-May, there was a spike in PVC, and that’s why you see in the
last year’s first quarter, the volume growth across industries was very good. We were also close to 16%
volume growth, and then it started a southward journey. This is a continuous falling graph, and last
year alone, the polymer price was down by 18%. Despite a 18% fall, you can see that in this
environment, Astral is the only company in the country that has improved its gross profit margin. We
have improved the GP by 1%, and we have maintained our EBITDA.
So this is the power of the brand. I can say that we have established ourselves in the market, where
people respect the brand, and people are ready to pay us for the quality. Many players have tried a lot
of things by undercutting the value and throwing the material in the market with material, but even
after that, they were not able to grow their volumes. So, customers understand that quality players
will always command a premium, and that’s what we are always communicating that we believe in
consistency. We don’t want zigzag growth. We want to maintain consistency in terms of growth and
margins as well. And that’s what we are doing right now. There were a lot of pressures on us too, with
competitors selling at lower prices, and now they are all suffering. Many of their projects are getting
blacklisted as well. We don’t want to name anyone, but everyone has started facing this problem in
the market.
This is a graph of paint and adhesive. You can see the last five years' growth; you can see 21.23% but
that’s not the correct graph because paint was added at a later stage. So, it may not be the right graph.
The EBITDA growth is 8.55%, and the profit before tax (PBT) is low because we have taken a lot of
amortizations that’s why it is showing very low Over the last three years, if you see, we have made a
lot of corrections. New additions are taking place for dealers, and because of that, EBITDA is under
pressure, particularly in the paint business. But now, I think, once our volumes start picking up from
this year itself, you will see a lot of improvement in this as well.
As far as the adhesive business is concerned, I think we are consistently growing at a 17 t0 18 % for
the last 11 years from where we have started our journey in 2014 and when we started that time our
EBITDA percentage was just 6% now from 6% journey to this year full year number we are close to
17% I am not talking about the last quarter where we had a closed to about 19% EBITDA but that was

exceptional year and we are confident that we will be delivering the good number from the UK also
from the current year itself.
So, this is a very small letter. So we are going to share this presentation to you. So you can go through
that also. So what is the industry outlook? What we are saying? So we we are of the view that the

fittings approved by German authorities for gas application lines. They also have fittings that
can be used for water lines, Jal Sena projects, various projects, and industrial piping systems.
Now we are getting four machines—three are coming, and one more will come later—for
making PE and PP pipes in our plants. The reason behind Al Aziz was to enter this product line
with a complete range of products, including pipes and fittings.
PTMT has also started getting a good response, and you can see the range here. So, I think we
are covering all the products, all the ranges, and we would be happier to answer a lot of
queries you have in mind. Because of this challenging year that passed, the businesses we
have extended—especially the paint, bathware business, and the new polymer businesses we
have entered into thank you, everyone, for coming here. Thank you for attending one more
this analyst meets after the fiscal year. If you have more questions, we will be available. Kairav
is here, my elder son; Hiranand Bhai, our Director and CFO; and Saumya is here, the younger
son. Thank you very much to all of you, and our team is here to answer your questions. Thank
you, everyone.
Mr. Hiranand Savlani Speaks
Good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Astral’s analyst meet. The results are in front of you, so I won’t
discuss individual numbers much. We have a few slides to present, and then we will give maximum
time to the question-and-answer session. This is the consolidated full-year performance. You can see
the last five-year revenue growth CAGR is 16.5%, and similarly, EBITDA growth is 10.48%. PBT is 7.15%.
PBT is mainly low because we have taken a lot of hits on the amortization of the new businesses which
we acquired. To take the tax advantage, we amortized a lot of things, so because of that, the PBT
numbers are low. Otherwise, cash profit, you will see, is more or less similar to the EBITDA level.
This is the working capital cycle. In the month of March, the rupee appreciated, so we paid off a lot of
creditors early to take advantage of the currency. Because of that, the creditor levels have gone down.
Otherwise, you can see that the debtor days and inventory days are more or less in the similar line,
with two to three days here and there. But that has come down in the month of April.
This is the cash position. Right now, we are sitting on a net cash of ₹464 crore. This is the revenue
breakup of our pipe plumbing—it’s still the pipe business contributing 72%, and paint and adhesive
contributing 28%. In the coming time, you will see this ratio will be a little toward the paint and
adhesive side because the base is very low.
This is the capacity chart. You can see that the capacity has been continuously growing. Though We
might not require that much capacity, but we are still continuously pumping money into capex. The
reason is simple that we want to decentralize our units, and we want to be a pan-India operation,
considering our vision for the next 10 to 15 years. Because if we don’t decentralize our units, with
polymer prices coming down day by day, the element of freight cost is increasing in terms of
percentage. In that case, the more and more decentralized you are, the more you will get the
advantage of logistics costs. Considering that, we are continuously adding capacity. You might see our
average utilization in the 55 to 65 range, but once our decentralization is over, you will see a sizable
improvement. In the last two years alone, we have pumped in close to ₹1000 crore into this expansion
activity, particularly capex. But that benefit you will get in the next five years. So, capacity building is
taking place at a 10.3% CAGR, while sales are happening at a 13.5% CAGR. Capacity utilization is
continuously improving, but it’s still not up to the mark because we are decentralizing that thing.

Management Commentary:
So I think we can take any other questions. And thank you everyone. For joining us for this end of
this fiscal understanding from us. And discussing how the future is going to shape up for the years to
for this next fiscal. And everything is great Bright. And we are keeping on the hard work. And looking
forward again to meet you at the end of the next fiscal. Thank you very much everyone. Thank you.
Thank you.

results.question: What is the computed ROCE and the computed cost of debt (interest expense / total borrowings) for the most recent 2 financial years? Is the company earning above its cost of borrowing?
results.expected_answer: FY25: ROCE=19.32%, Cost of Debt=17.71%, Spread=+1.61%. FY26: ROCE=18.45%, Cost of Debt=25.73%, Spread=-7.28%. Company earned above cost of debt in FY25 but NOT in FY26.
results.answer:
## Details

### Return on Capital Employed (ROCE)

ROCE is generally calculated as:

\[
\text{ROCE} = \frac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Capital Employed}} \times 100
\]

Where:
- EBIT = Profit before interest and tax = Profit before tax + Interest expense
- Capital Employed = Total borrowings + Equity (or Total Assets - Current Liabilities, but exact components must be from context)

From the context, we have:

**For FY2026 (most recent year):**

- Profit before tax = ₹730.60 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY26)
- Interest expense = ₹64.40 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY26)
- EBIT = Profit before tax + Interest = 730.60 + 64.40 = ₹795.00 Cr

- Total borrowings as of March 31, 2025 (FY25 year-end) = ₹9,991 million = ₹999.1 Cr (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 154 · FY25)
- Total borrowings as of March 31, 2024 (FY24 year-end) = ₹8,719 million = ₹871.9 Cr (same source)

Note: Borrowings for FY26 are not explicitly given, so we use FY25 borrowings as proxy for FY26 cost of debt calculation.

Capital Employed is not explicitly given. Since equity or total assets are not provided in the retrieved context, we cannot compute exact capital employed. Therefore, ROCE cannot be precisely calculated.

---

### Cost of Debt

Cost of debt is calculated as:

\[
\text{Cost of Debt} = \frac{\text{Interest Expense}}{\text{Total Borrowings}} \times 100
\]

Using the available data:

**FY2025:**

- Interest expense = ₹41.30 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25)
- Total borrowings = ₹9,991 million = ₹999.1 Cr (Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 154 · FY25)

\[
\text{Cost of Debt FY25} = \frac{41.30}{999.1} \times 100 = 4.13\%
\]

**FY2026:**

- Interest expense = ₹64.40 Cr (Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY26)
- Total borrowings for FY26 not disclosed; last known is FY25 borrowings ₹999.1 Cr

Since FY26 borrowings are not disclosed, cost of debt for FY26 cannot be precisely calculated.

---

### Is the company earning above its cost of borrowing?

To check if the company earns above its cost of borrowing, compare EBIT margin or ROCE with cost of debt.

- EBIT for FY25 = Profit before tax + Interest = ₹702.50 Cr + ₹41.30 Cr = ₹743.80 Cr
- Sales FY25 = ₹5832.40 Cr

EBIT margin FY25 = (743.80 / 5832.40) × 100 = 12.75%

Cost of debt FY25 = 4.13%

Since EBIT margin (12.75%) is higher than cost of debt (4.13%), the company is earning above its cost of borrowing in FY25.

For FY26, EBIT margin and cost of debt cannot be precisely computed due to missing borrowings data.

---

## Summary

- Cost of debt for FY25 is 4.13% (Interest ₹41.30 Cr / Borrowings ₹999.1 Cr).
- Cost of debt for FY26 cannot be computed due to missing borrowings data.
- ROCE cannot be computed for FY25 or FY26 due to lack of capital employed data.
- The company’s EBIT margin in FY25 (12.75%) exceeds its cost of debt (4.13%), indicating it is earning above its cost of borrowing for FY25.
- For FY26, insufficient data prevents calculation or comparison.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25  
Astral_Ltd.xlsx · profit_loss · FY26  
Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Page 154 · FY25

chunks.content:
Astral Analyst Meet,2025
23rd May,2025
Mr. Sandeep Engineer Speaks
So, we welcome you all to this fiscal year-end results discussion, and we’ll also talk about the
future and scenarios. We’ll answer all your questions in person. So, welcome all of you.
Actually, the range is so vast and significant that we opted for a bigger hall so that everybody
can see the complete range of product lines. We are also having a dealer meet thereafter from
8 PM in the same hall for Mumbai.
This year has been a very challenging year. The questions I’ve been answering since morning,
especially about the polymer industry, indicate that everyone faced a big, challenging year
and PVC was the most challenging polymer, where price challenges were prominent, followed
by growth challenges. But in this scenario, as you can see, across all four quarters, we have
delivered very good results. The growth has been modest, but the margins have been well-
maintained and improved.
From this, you can see many questions were asked about our CPVC growth. We don’t want to
quantify it people have quantified their numbers in percentages, above a certain percentage
but we don’t want to quantify the numbers. If you look at the numbers and the margins,
obviously, the market share and the growth of value-added products have gone up. CPVC is
one of the value-added products, as are the new products. If people have quantified the
numbers with huge growth, we don’t see any value in quantified numbers that reflect in value
addition. In short, I’m saying we have achieved growth in our core product lines and new
product lines, and we’ve done this with healthy margins.
As usual, I don’t want to go into a presentation or discuss capex cycles because this will be
covered in value terms. Mr. Savlani will take you through these numbers. But every plant has
a balance of expansion going on, including new plants. The Hyderabad plant is fully
operational, and this year, we will have the full range of pipe products manufactured from the
Hyderabad plant. The Kanpur plant is on the verge of completion in the construction segment,
and machines will start arriving from the next quarter. So, in the third quarter, the Kanpur
plant will be operational, and by year-end, it will be fully operational.
We have started fitting operations in the South and also in Rajasthan. We will shortly start
fitting operations in the East, in Orissa. There are also expansion plants in progress, so we can
easily cater to those markets for pipes as well as fittings. Our new range of valves, especially
ball valves, is complete. We are adding butterfly valves and other types of valves used in the
plumbing segment. This range of specialized valve products will be completed by this fiscal
year.
We are also adding machines for our silent pipe production. We have almost doubled the
capacity, even more than doubled. Our Drain Pro, which is doing well excellently, has also

Analyst (Attendee) asked:
Hello hi good evening this is Keshav from HDFC securities sir we are anticipating 10 to 15% volume
growth for next year at the same time we feel UK operation will improve paint operation margin can
improve but while we have seen Hiranand bhai interview he have mentioned the margin will remain
stable in FY26 why are we not expecting some kind of improvement.

Management answered:
I have never ever said margin is going to stable. I said the margin of India operation is going to remain
stable. This world whatever the new businesses are there that will be top up to that. So paint we have
said that it will be small improvement will be there this year also. UK has not given a single penny this
year zero EBITA. So how can be same next year? Historically you see they were delivering 8 to 9%
minimum EBITA. So margin on consol basis it is going to improve and secondly never ever see the last
quarter number only we have to consider the full year number and we have to account the inventory
losses also now next year suppose now more or less PVC is getting bottom out ok that is what our
view we may be wrong also so dont dont go with my view only but we are of the view that the PVC is
slowly and visually going to bottom out upward journey may take some time also or make take quickly
also. It depend on this two outcome of anti dumping NBI. In that case next year you may see inventory
gain also. We have deliver in 24 EBITA also in particular quarter. So very very difficult to say thats why
we are giving you a long term range that we will be working between 16 to 18% in the pipe category
and adhesives 14 to 16 we have communicated. And last 3 year number forget about current last 3
year number if you see adhesive India business have given close to about 15.5% EBITA margin so we
can give you only range which may not be holding good depend on the Circumstances and now
volatility is very high which was not the case earlier earlier the PVC price range was between 5 or 7
plus minus now it is 20 or 27 % plus minus also so any company de cant give the exact guidance also
very difficult to predict the number

taken multiple measures in the US and UK, which will be reflected in the next two quarters in
terms of results. I don’t want to take up time and detail the measures we’ve taken, but as we
see the corrections, we will always discuss them with you.
Secondly the biggest benefit we get from the UK and US plants is that a lot of chemistries have
come from there. Our chemistry for silicones and our chemistry for certain adhesives,
including hybrids, have come from there. In the UK, we’ve gone four steps backward in
hybrids, which will improve margins in both the UK and India.
We’re coming up with a high-tech machine with the help of US and UK technologists and R&D
people. It will be the first to produce 100 colours of different cartridges coloured silicone for
the first time in India. There will be 100 different colours of silicones offered in the market by
us. So, there are a lot of positive things. There’s nothing great about negativity, but there’s
great potential in the growth and margins, which we will address very diligently this year.
Coming to the paint segment, because paint is another area with a lot of worries, I understand.
But if you look at it another way, we are very small-scale in paint. Again, I want to tell everyone
that paint was specifically acquired to increase the adhesive business, to be in the paint
business, and also in the industrial paint and construction chemical business. We haven’t put
any capex behind the paint business. When we acquired it last year, we said the capacity is
sufficient, and we won’t be adding any capex, which remains the case.
Last year, the concern was about burning money. Paint has a positive EBITDA, though it’s low,
and there’s nothing negative. The growth is low. The reason is that after last March, when we
took over, when we took over 80% and we took over Control of the paint business. Until then,
two quarters went into multiple corrections, and the same continued in the last quarter. We
achieved good growth in paint, and we continue to see good growth in paint. As the volumes
increase, obviously, the margins will improve. We launched paint in the first and second
quarters in Gujarat, in many cities. Now Astral Paint has started selling, moving well, growing,

results.question: What are the key revenue drivers and priorities outlined by management for the coming year?
results.expected_answer: In the FY25 Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani outlined the key revenue drivers/priorities for the coming year: ramping up utilization at the three newly opened plants (Guwahati, Bhubaneswar, Hyderabad); continued focus on 'increasing value-added products' (higher-than-normal EBITDA margin products); value-added launches such as fire-pro pipes (post UL fitting approval) and OPVC; growing exports via the newly opened Dubai overseas office targeting 'Middle East, Saudi Arabia, or select African markets' and Europe; scaling the newly acquired Al Aziz electrofusion fittings/PE-PP pipe business, including gas application pipes as a long-term opportunity; continued R&D spend, including a new silicone range with '100 colour options'; and expanding the adhesives business into rural India via the 'New Bharat concept.' Sandeep Engineer separately emphasized correcting/reviving the UK and US adhesive businesses over 'the next two to three quarters.'
results.answer:
## Details

From the PDF annual report narrative sections (Sources [1], [6], and [9]):

- The industry is expected to have broadly stable volume growth at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 9-10% through to 2030.
- Key revenue drivers include:
  - Broader urban expansion.
  - Rising construction activity in tier-2 and tier-3 cities.
  - Growing consumer shift towards premium and functional products.
  - Sustainability trends influencing product offerings.
- Management highlights challenges such as pricing pressures due to intensified promotional activity and increased competition, especially in the decorative segment.
- The UK adhesives market remains subdued, particularly in the silicone segment, affected by macroeconomic headwinds.
- Management’s priorities for the coming year include:
  - Driving efficiency across procurement, manufacturing, and logistics.
  - Continued investment in product innovation and process improvements to offset margin pressures.
  - Disciplined capital allocation focusing on projects with attractive returns and long-term growth potential.
  - Investment in brands, technology, and operational excellence.
  - Balancing revenue growth with financial prudence.

## Summary

Management outlines key revenue drivers as urban expansion, increased construction in tier-2 and tier-3 cities, a consumer shift to premium and functional products, and sustainability trends. Their priorities for the coming year focus on operational efficiency, product innovation, disciplined capital allocation, and investments in brand and technology to sustain growth while managing margin pressures.

## Sources

Astral_Ltd_AR_FY2025_AnnualReport.pdf · Pages 1, 6, 9 · FY25

results.question: What factors did management attribute the company's performance to in the most recent quarter?
results.expected_answer: For Q3 FY25 (the most recent standalone quarterly earnings call in the provided documents), Sandeep Engineer attributed performance to: overall demand challenges 'due to overall infrastructure spending by the government and liquidity challenges in the market'; despite this, consolidated top line grew a 'marginal... 2%' with EBITDA growth of '9.3%' YoY; pipe vertical 'maintained our volume and grow our EBITDA by 10.9% in spite of huge volatility in polymer prices'; the anti-dumping duty on PVC 'got delayed, which also affected the industry volume growth'; Adhesives India delivered 14.5% growth with 16.4% EBITDA; Paint grew 7.5% with 4% EBITDA; overseas adhesive (UK/US) de-grew 5% but held positive EBITDA of 0.7-1%.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (AstralConcall25Q4.pdf), management attributed the company's recent quarter performance to several factors:

1. **Polymer Price Decline Impact**: Polymer prices collapsed by 18% in the financial year, which weakened the top line and caused employee cost and other expenses as a percentage of sales to balloon (Page 13).

2. **Business Expansion and Manpower Costs**: The company increased manpower in new and scaling businesses such as faucet ceramic ware, paint business, and geographic expansions (Gujarat, Rajasthan). This initial scaling led to higher manpower costs, especially at the lower end, though some corrections were made (Page 12).

3. **Capacity Expansion and Decentralization**: The company has been investing heavily in capacity expansion (close to ₹1000 crore in the last two years) to decentralize operations pan-India, which is expected to improve logistics costs and capacity utilization over the next five years. Current utilization is 55-65%, improving but still below optimal (Page 6).

4. **Segment Mix Shift**: Pipes still contribute 72% of revenue, with paint and adhesive at 28%. The management expects the paint and adhesive share to increase over time due to a low base (Page 6).

5. **Margin Stability and Improvement Outlook**: Management clarified that India operations margins are expected to remain stable, with small improvements in paint margins. The UK operation had zero EBITA this year but historically delivered 8-9% EBITA, so consolidated margins are expected to improve (Page 19).

6. **Inventory and Currency Effects**: Inventory losses and currency appreciation affected working capital and creditor payments, with some creditor levels reduced due to early payments to take advantage of currency movements (Page 6).

7. **Digital and Supply Chain Initiatives**: The company is implementing digital initiatives such as warehouse management software and auto-refilling systems for distributors and dealers to improve operational efficiencies and reduce manpower needs in order taking (Page 21).

## Summary

Management attributed the company's recent quarter performance primarily to the sharp decline in polymer prices impacting top line and cost ratios, increased manpower costs due to business scaling and geographic expansion, ongoing capacity expansion and decentralization efforts, stable margins in India with expected small improvements in paint, zero EBITA from UK operations this year, inventory and currency effects on working capital, and digital initiatives aimed at improving supply chain efficiency.

## Sources

AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Pages 6, 12, 13, 19, 21 · FY25

results.question: What near-term risks or challenges did management highlight during the calls?
results.expected_answer: Management highlighted several near-term risks/challenges across the calls: (1) uncertainty on timing/quantum of the PVC anti-dumping duty implementation - Hiranand Savlani (Q3 call): 'we don't know when it is going to be announced by the government'; (2) uncertainty on BIS standard implementation timing - referenced as pending in Q3/Q2 calls; (3) subpar/uncertain channel inventory levels - Hiranand Savlani (Q3 call): 'it is subpar. Because so much of volatility there, nobody has a confidence'; (4) polymer/PVC price volatility - Sandeep Engineer (Analyst Meet): PVC price fell 18% in FY25; (5) government infra spending slowdown and liquidity challenges facing contractors - discussed by Hiranand Savlani in the Analyst Meet; (6) UK adhesive business underperformance and manpower/executive performance issues - Sandeep Engineer (Q2 call); (7) potential US tariff war impact - though Hiranand Savlani (Q3 call) stated 'we are not exporting anything to the US market... I don't think this tariff is going to affect anyway to our company.'
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcripts (AstralConcall25Q4.pdf), management highlighted several near-term risks and challenges:

1. **Volatility in PVC Prices**  
   - PVC prices have shown high volatility recently, with price ranges moving between 20% to 27% plus or minus, compared to a previous range of 5% to 7%.  
   - This volatility makes it very difficult to predict margins or exact financial outcomes.  
   - Management mentioned that PVC prices are "slowly and visually going to bottom out," but the upward journey could take time or happen quickly depending on external factors like anti-dumping duties and NBI decisions.  
   - Inventory losses or gains related to PVC price movements are also a risk factor affecting margins.

2. **Supply Chain and Market Maturity in New Businesses**  
   - The paint business and other new segments are still maturing, and management acknowledged it will take time to fully understand and optimize supply chain operations in these areas.  
   - They are working on digital initiatives like auto-refilling of distributor orders to improve efficiency, but these are ongoing efforts.

3. **Competitive Market Environment**  
   - Management noted increased competitiveness in the market, which has led them to stop giving specific growth guidance for new product lines.  
   - This competitive pressure is a challenge for growth and margin expansion.

4. **Capacity Utilization and Decentralization**  
   - The company is investing heavily in capacity expansion and decentralization to reduce logistics costs, but current utilization is only around 55-65%, which is below optimal levels.  
   - This implies near-term pressure on profitability until utilization improves.

5. **Uncertainty in Regulatory Decisions**  
   - Outcomes of anti-dumping and NBI (likely regulatory or trade-related decisions) are critical and uncertain, impacting PVC pricing and inventory valuation.

6. **Employee and Other Expenses as Percentage of Sales**  
   - Due to polymer price declines eroding top-line growth, employee costs and other expenses as a percentage of sales have ballooned, which is a cost-efficiency challenge.

## Summary

Management highlighted near-term risks including high volatility in PVC prices affecting margins and inventory valuation, ongoing supply chain and market maturity challenges in new businesses like paints, increased market competitiveness limiting growth visibility, suboptimal capacity utilization amid decentralization efforts, and uncertainty around regulatory decisions impacting raw material costs. Additionally, cost pressures from employee and other expenses rising as a percentage of sales due to top-line erosion were noted as challenges.

## Sources

AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Pages 4, 11, 19, 20, 21 · FY25

Good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to Astral’s analyst meet. The results are in front of you, so I won’t

breakup of our pipe plumbing—it’s still the pipe business contributing 72%, and paint and adhesive
contributing 28%. In the coming time, you will see this ratio will be a little toward the paint and
adhesive side because the base is very low.
This is the capacity chart. You can see that the capacity has been continuously growing. Though We
might not require that much capacity, but we are still continuously pumping money into capex. The
reason is simple that we want to decentralize our units, and we want to be a pan-India operation,
considering our vision for the next 10 to 15 years. Because if we don’t decentralize our units, with
polymer prices coming down day by day, the element of freight cost is increasing in terms of
percentage. In that case, the more and more decentralized you are, the more you will get the
advantage of logistics costs. Considering that, we are continuously adding capacity. You might see our
average utilization in the 55 to 65 range, but once our decentralization is over, you will see a sizable
improvement. In the last two years alone, we have pumped in close to ₹1000 crore into this expansion
activity, particularly capex. But that benefit you will get in the next five years. So, capacity building is
taking place at a 10.3% CAGR, while sales are happening at a 13.5% CAGR. Capacity utilization is
continuously improving, but it’s still not up to the mark because we are decentralizing that thing.

results.question: What is management's volume and margin guidance for the core business divisions?
results.expected_answer: Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, in response to Sneha Talreja/Shravan Shah): 'our normal range will be between 10% to 15% in the volume... next year also we are expecting that we should be definitely going to deliver between 10% to 15% minimum'; pipe margin guidance range reaffirmed at 16%-18% ('I think my range will remain same'); adhesive India margin guidance '14 to 16 we have given the Indian operation'; UK adhesive margin guidance from Sandeep Engineer: 'UK margin will bring back to that 5% to 10%, which has been a historical margin.' In the Q2 FY25 call, Hiranand Savlani guided: 'We are expecting this year to end between 10% to 15% volume growth' and consolidated EBITDA guidance of '15% to 16%.' No specific capacity utilization percentage guidance for next year was given.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (AstralConcall25Q4.pdf):

- **Volume Guidance:**
  - Management expects volume growth largely depends on the timing of the Anti-Dumping Duty (ATD) announcement and finalization of BIS norms.
  - Currently, channel inventory is very low.
  - If ATD and BIS announcements come by end of Q1 or beginning of Q2, volume growth in subsequent quarters could be in the mid to high double digits.
  - Optimally, management is aiming for a **lower double-digit volume growth** for the year.
  - If market conditions improve (PVC price movement and market sentiment), they may over-deliver on this projection.

- **Margin Guidance:**
  - For the India operations (core business), management expects margins to remain **stable**.
  - Paint and UK operations are new businesses and expected to add to consolidated margins.
  - UK operation had zero EBITA this year but historically delivered 8-9% EBITA; hence consolidated margin is expected to improve.
  - For the pipe category, management targets an EBITDA margin range of **16-18%**.
  - For adhesives (India business), the EBITA margin range communicated is **14-16%**.
  - Management cautions that due to high volatility in PVC prices, exact margin guidance is difficult, but they expect to work within these ranges.
  - They do not expect margin deterioration and see potential for small improvements in paint margins.

## Summary

Management's volume guidance for the core business is a **lower double-digit percentage growth** for the year, potentially higher if key regulatory decisions (ATD and BIS norms) come early. Margin guidance for the core pipe business is stable with an expected EBITDA margin range of **16-18%**, and adhesives margin is expected in the **14-16%** range, with consolidated margins likely to improve due to new businesses like paint and UK operations.

## Sources

AstralConcall25Q4.pdf · Pages 6-7, 11, 16, 19 · FY25

automatically the volume numbers will move in a much faster manner.

Management Commentary:
Generally, it is 50-50

always be low-capacity utilization. We have to create a dealer and distributor network. Now that is
happening. Once that is in place, these plants’ utilization will improve substantially, and that is going
to drive the business to the next level because there are a lot of savings in logistics costs. That is going
to help us in a big way in the coming time.
Secondly, our focus is continuously on increasing value-added products. Value-added products mean
products that have higher-than-normal EBITDA margins. Our focus is continuously to add these kinds
of products. I think a few of the products have already been discussed by Sandeep Bhai, so I don’t want
to discuss much on that side. But our focus is continuously going in that direction because we know
some products may not give high volume, but they will give high value addition, which will help
maintain margins in these difficult times. When the polymer price was down by a 18%, we were still
able to maintain our EBITDA margin, mainly because of this value addition.
On the value addition side, we are looking at fire-pro pipes because recently we got the fitting
approval. We already had the pipe approval from UL, and now we got the fittings, so that will open up
the door for the export market as well as the local market. We were too dependent on the import of
fittings and other things, so that will stop, and now we will indigenously start manufacturing
everything. That is going to help us with margins as well as growth. Similarly, OPVC is another product
that also has high margins. We are expecting that volume to improve as well.
Thirdly, we have recently opened our first overseas office in Dubai. We are targeting that all these
value-added products’ contribution should increase from those geographies as well may be the Middle
East, Saudi Arabia, or select African markets. We are exporting some products to Europe as well. We
are expecting that in the coming time, our focus team will be focusing on these value-added products.
Even Dubai alone has very high-tech projects coming up. They may also like Astral products because
they are world-class products. They are not only approved in India because we all know ISI approval is
not that stringent compared to UL approval. UL approval involves keeping a single pipe at a certain
temperature for 18 months. If nothing happens to the pipe for up to 18 months, only then do they give
approval. So, it’s a very stringent approval. That’s why you see none of the Indian companies ever
trying to go for UL approval. Our products are world-class. We have high expectations that now we
have just entered the export market, which is also going to drive the next level of growth.
The shift from unorganized to organized, we already discussed, and BIS is going to help us. Another
recent addition is Al Aziz products, as Sandeep Bhai already discussed it’s an electrofusion fitting. That
was a range that was not in our basket. We have already placed the order for the machine for pipes,
and now they will support us in fittings. So, we will be able to complete the range. That is also going
to help us grow that product in the Indian territory, and there is a lot of scope for the export market
for these electrofusion fittings as well. There is high demand in the international market too. We are
just exploring. We are shortly going to meet a few export clients, and we will get a sense of their
expectations. They were trying to export, but due to liquidity concerns, they were not able to complete
those orders. Now we are going to sit with them, and we will see how best we can quickly revive that
relationship and target the export market for these Al Aziz products also.
Another new thing is that we are continuously spending money on the R&D side. That will help us
develop a lot of new things in the coming time. You can see that from the day Astral started its journey
until today, almost every year we are bringing something new to the table, and that continues today.
We are targeting that in the coming time, we will bring a few new products, and you will see that Astral
is the company that is bringing many products to the country for the first time. So far, we have brought
seven to eight products that we introduced for the first time in the country.
